title: LVAD Alarm Management
subtitle: Clinical Response Protocols
description: Comprehensive alarm interpretation and clinical decision-making for bedside nursing staff.

items.title: Low Flow Advisory
items.content: Flow <2.0 L/min sustained. Assess volume status, patient position, MAP. Check for suction event. Review recent fluid balance.

items.title: Low Battery Advisory
items.content: Battery life <30 minutes remaining on one or both batteries. Immediate battery exchange or connect to power module.

items.title: Low Speed Advisory
items.content: RPM below prescribed minimum. Assess controller integrity, driveline connection, and power source.

steps.title: Assess Patient
steps.content: Immediately assess patient responsiveness, perfusion status, and vital signs. Do NOT silence alarm without VAD team notification.

steps.title: Call VAD Team
steps.content: Contact VAD coordinator or on-call physician immediately. Describe alarm type, patient status, and current device parameters.

steps.title: Check Connections
steps.content: Verify all connections: batteries to controller, controller to driveline. Ensure no disconnections or kinks.

steps.title: Document Parameters
steps.content: Record: flow, speed (RPM), pulsatility index (PI), power consumption, and MAP. Time-stamp all interventions.

steps.title: Controller Exchange
steps.content: If directed by VAD team, perform emergency controller exchange per your institution's protocol. Have backup controller at bedside at all times.

sections.title: Suction Event Recognition
sections.content: A suction event occurs when the LVAD inlet is sucked against the ventricular wall due to hypovolemia, excessive pump speed, or RV failure. Signs include: fluctuating flow, low PI, intermittent low-flow alarms. Treatment: fluid bolus 250–500mL, speed reduction (per VAD team), position change to left lateral decubitus.

quiz.question: A patient's LVAD is alarming red continuously. What is your FIRST action?
options: Silence the alarm and assess the controller
Assess patient status and call the VAD team immediately
Document the event in the chart first
quiz.explanation: Patient assessment and immediate VAD team notification is always the first priority for a red hazard alarm. Never silence a red alarm without clinical evaluation.

quiz.question: A suction event is most likely caused by which of the following?
quiz.explanation: Suction events most commonly result from hypovolemia (low preload), excessive pump speed, or right ventricular failure reducing flow to the left ventricle.

quiz.question: What flow level triggers a Low Flow Advisory alarm?
quiz.explanation: A sustained flow less than 2.0 L/min typically triggers a Low Flow Advisory (yellow) alarm requiring clinical assessment.

quiz.question: When is it acceptable to silence a red hazard alarm without contacting the VAD team?
options: True — if the patient appears comfortable
quiz.explanation: It is never acceptable to silence a red hazard alarm without contacting the VAD team and performing a full clinical assessment.

quiz.question: Initial treatment for a suspected suction event includes:
options: Fluid bolus and position change to left lateral decubitus
quiz.explanation: Initial management of a suction event includes a fluid bolus (250–500mL), reducing pump speed per VAD team guidance, and positioning the patient left lateral decubitus to increase preload.
